subtopic_detail: Severe DKA initial fluid and insulin timing
stem:
A 7-year-old boy is brought to a regional emergency department by his mother after three days of polydipsia, polyuria, and weight loss followed by twelve hours of vomiting and progressive lethargy. He has no prior diagnosis of diabetes. He has no headache and no focal neurology.

On examination he is mildly drowsy but rouseable with eyes-open-to-voice and engages in short sentences. Mucous membranes are dry, capillary return is three seconds, and respirations are deep and slow. Pulse rate 144, blood pressure 98/58, respiratory rate 32, SpO2 99 percent on room air, temperature 36.8 degrees, weight 22 kg. Capillary glucose is 28 mmol/L. The retrieval team will arrive in 60 minutes for transfer to the tertiary paediatric centre.

data_table.Investigations: venous blood gas pH 7.06, bicarbonate 7 mmol/L (22-28), base excess negative 22, sodium 134 mmol/L (135-145), potassium 5.4 mmol/L (3.5-5.0), urea 9.2 mmol/L (2.5-7.8), creatinine 88 micromol/L, beta-hydroxybutyrate 5.2 mmol/L, GCS 14 (E3 V5 M6)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate fluid and insulin plan over the first hour?

options.text: Administer 20 mL/kg 0.9 percent saline as a rapid bolus over fifteen minutes and start intravenous insulin 0.1 units/kg/hour immediately after the bolus
options.rationale: Rapid 20 mL/kg bolus plus immediate insulin in severe paediatric DKA increases cerebral oedema risk through abrupt osmotic shift; the Australasian Paediatric Endocrine Group and ISPAD recommend slower initial fluid replacement and delayed insulin in severe DKA. (Source: APEG Paediatric DKA Guideline)

options.text: Administer 10 mL/kg 0.9 percent saline over thirty minutes, defer insulin until one hour after fluids commence, and arrange immediate paediatric retrieval
options.rationale: Severe DKA (pH less than 7.1, GCS depressed) in a child carries the highest cerebral oedema risk. APEG and ISPAD recommend cautious 10 mL/kg bolus over 30 to 60 minutes plus delayed insulin (start at 1 to 2 hours after fluids) at 0.05 units/kg/hour to reduce osmotic shift, and urgent paediatric retrieval. (Source: APEG Paediatric DKA Guideline)

options.text: Administer maintenance 0.9 percent saline at 0.5 mL/kg/hour and commence intravenous sodium bicarbonate 1 mmol/kg to correct severe acidosis
options.rationale: Sodium bicarbonate in paediatric DKA is associated with worse outcomes including a higher rate of cerebral oedema and is not recommended outside refractory acidosis with cardiovascular compromise unresponsive to fluids. Maintenance-only fluids are inadequate. (Source: APEG Paediatric DKA Guideline)

options.text: Administer 20 mL/kg Hartmann's solution as a rapid bolus and commence intravenous insulin 0.05 units/kg/hour with added potassium 40 mmol/L
options.rationale: Hartmann's and rapid 20 mL/kg are not the recommended initial replacement in severe paediatric DKA; 0.9 percent saline at a slower rate is standard. Insulin commenced concurrently with bolus carries cerebral oedema risk. (Source: APEG Paediatric DKA Guideline)

options.text: Administer subcutaneous insulin 0.3 units/kg as a stat dose and offer oral rehydration with 10 mL/kg over the next sixty minutes
options.rationale: Subcutaneous insulin is unreliable in shock states and oral rehydration is inappropriate in a vomiting child with reduced GCS at risk of aspiration. Intravenous insulin is mandatory once fluids are running. (Source: APEG Paediatric DKA Guideline)

explanation.summary: Severe paediatric DKA (pH < 7.1, depressed conscious state) carries the highest cerebral oedema risk. APEG and ISPAD recommend a cautious initial 10 mL/kg 0.9 percent saline bolus over 30 to 60 minutes and deliberate insulin delay (start at 1 to 2 hours after fluids commence) at 0.05 units/kg/hour, with paediatric retrieval and tertiary transfer. Faster fluid resuscitation and earlier insulin are associated with worse neurological outcomes.
key_points: Severe DKA in children: cautious 10 mL/kg 0.9 percent saline over 30-60 min
Delay insulin start by 1-2 hours after fluid commences
Insulin infusion at 0.05 units/kg/hour, not 0.1 units/kg/hour
Avoid sodium bicarbonate (worsens cerebral oedema outcomes)
Cerebral oedema warning signs: headache, bradycardia with hypertension, falling GCS
explanation.pearls: In a child with DKA and pH below 7.1 the insulin infusion runs at 0.05 units/kg/hour and only starts 1 to 2 hours after the cautious 10 mL/kg fluid bolus has commenced to minimise osmotic shifts that precede cerebral oedema.

stem:
A 32-year-old G3P3 has just had a spontaneous vaginal delivery at 39+5 weeks of a 4.1 kg baby with rapid third stage. Her booking pregnancy was complicated by pregnancy-induced hypertension and she has been on oral labetalol 200 mg three times daily since 32 weeks with home blood pressures around 138/86. Her placenta delivered apparently intact ten minutes ago.

On assessment now her uterus is boggy at the umbilicus despite fundal massage and she has had 800 mL of fresh red blood loss into the drape in the last 15 minutes. The midwife has called for senior review and a second wide-bore intravenous line is being placed. She is alert but pale and clammy. The placenta has been re-examined and looks complete with intact cotyledons and membranes. There is no vaginal or perineal laceration on inspection.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse rate 110, blood pressure 95/60, respiratory rate 22, SpO2 98 percent on room air, temperature 36.8 degrees

lead_in: Which uterotonic regimen is most appropriate?

options.text: Ergometrine 250 micrograms intramuscular as first line and a 40 unit oxytocin infusion in 1000 mL Hartmann's over four hours
options.rationale: Ergometrine is contraindicated in pregnancy-induced hypertension because of severe rebound vasoconstriction and stroke risk; an alternative uterotonic must be used here. (Source: RANZCOG: Postpartum Haemorrhage)

options.text: Sublingual misoprostol 800 micrograms with intrauterine vaginal packing in the delivery room while awaiting senior review
options.rationale: Misoprostol is a second-line uterotonic when oxytocin fails. Intrauterine packing is not recommended ahead of oxytocin escalation and a Bakri balloon. (Source: RANZCOG: Postpartum Haemorrhage)

options.text: Carboprost 250 micrograms intramuscular every fifteen minutes for up to eight doses as primary uterotonic for atony
options.rationale: Carboprost is reserved for refractory uterine atony and is contraindicated in asthma. It is not the first uterotonic to escalate to when oxytocin has not yet been used at therapeutic doses. (Source: RANZCOG: Postpartum Haemorrhage)

options.text: Oxytocin 10 units intramuscular plus a 40 unit oxytocin infusion in 1000 mL Hartmann's over four hours, and intravenous tranexamic acid 1 g
options.rationale: Oxytocin is first line for postpartum haemorrhage and is safe in pregnancy-induced hypertension. Tranexamic acid 1 g within three hours of onset reduces mortality per the WOMAN trial and is endorsed by RANZCOG. (Source: RANZCOG: Postpartum Haemorrhage)

options.text: Bakri balloon insertion at the bedside and immediate transfer to the operating theatre for examination under anaesthesia
options.rationale: Mechanical tamponade and theatre are reserved for failed medical management with ongoing haemorrhage. Stepwise escalation begins with oxytocin and tranexamic acid. (Source: RANZCOG: Postpartum Haemorrhage)

explanation.summary: First-line medical management of atonic postpartum haemorrhage in Australia is oxytocin (10 units intramuscular plus 40 unit infusion in 1000 mL crystalloid over four hours) plus 1 g intravenous tranexamic acid within three hours of onset. Ergometrine is a useful second-line agent but is contraindicated in pregnancy-induced hypertension. Misoprostol, carboprost (avoid in asthma), mechanical tamponade, and surgical escalation follow if haemorrhage continues despite oxytocin.
key_points: Oxytocin is first line for atonic postpartum haemorrhage
Tranexamic acid 1 g intravenous within 3 hours reduces mortality (WOMAN trial)
Ergometrine is contraindicated in hypertension and pre-eclampsia
Carboprost is contraindicated in asthma
Misoprostol, Bakri balloon, and laparotomy escalate sequentially if oxytocin fails
explanation.pearls: In a woman with pregnancy-induced hypertension or pre-eclampsia the second-line uterotonic switches from ergometrine (contraindicated) to carboprost (still contraindicated in asthma) and then misoprostol, before mechanical and surgical escalation.

subtopic_detail: Intrapartum GBS prophylaxis with penicillin allergy
stem:
A 28-year-old G2P1 at 38+0 weeks presents to delivery suite in early labour. Her vaginal-rectal screening swab at 36 weeks grew group B streptococcus. She has a previous vaginal delivery at 39 weeks without complications. Her first child had no neonatal sepsis. She is otherwise well.

She tells you she developed a generalised pruritic rash and lip swelling when prescribed amoxicillin two years ago for tonsillitis and was told to avoid penicillins by the emergency department doctor at the time. She has not had any antibiotic since then. She is currently 4 cm dilated with regular contractions and ruptured membranes one hour ago at home. The midwife has commenced intravenous access and a syntocinon augmentation has not yet been started.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse rate 92, blood pressure 118/76, respiratory rate 18, SpO2 99 percent on room air, temperature 36.9 degrees, foetal heart rate 142 with normal variability

lead_in: Which intrapartum antibiotic regimen is most appropriate?

options.text: Intravenous clindamycin 600 mg every eight hours from now until delivery, confirming GBS susceptibility on the laboratory antibiogram
options.rationale: Severe penicillin allergy (anaphylaxis or angioedema) precludes both penicillin and cefazolin. Clindamycin is recommended if the GBS isolate is documented as clindamycin susceptible; otherwise vancomycin is used. (Source: RANZCOG: Maternal Group B Streptococcus)

options.text: Intravenous cefazolin 2 g loading dose then 1 g every eight hours until delivery, after a graded penicillin challenge in labour
options.rationale: Cefazolin is acceptable for mild non-anaphylactic penicillin allergy but is contraindicated when the index reaction is severe (angioedema or anaphylaxis). Graded challenge is not appropriate in active labour. (Source: RANZCOG: Maternal Group B Streptococcus)

options.text: Intravenous benzylpenicillin 3 g loading dose then 1.8 g every four hours until delivery, with adrenaline drawn up at the bedside
options.rationale: Benzylpenicillin is contraindicated in confirmed severe penicillin allergy regardless of pre-drawn adrenaline. (Source: RANZCOG: Maternal Group B Streptococcus)

options.text: Oral cefalexin 500 mg every six hours until delivery and a single intravenous dose at the time of delivery to cover the neonate
options.rationale: Oral antibiotics do not achieve adequate amniotic fluid levels in time for intrapartum GBS prophylaxis. Intravenous administration is required. (Source: RANZCOG: Maternal Group B Streptococcus)

options.text: No antibiotics required because she had a previous uncomplicated vaginal delivery and the colony count is unknown
options.rationale: A positive screening swab at 35 to 37 weeks is itself an indication for intrapartum prophylaxis regardless of previous delivery course or colony count. (Source: RANZCOG: Maternal Group B Streptococcus)

explanation.summary: RANZCOG recommends intrapartum GBS prophylaxis with intravenous benzylpenicillin (or ampicillin) for women with a positive 35 to 37 week vaginal-rectal swab, previous GBS infant, GBS bacteriuria during pregnancy, intrapartum fever, or membranes ruptured 18 hours or more. For severe penicillin allergy, clindamycin is used if the isolate is susceptible; otherwise vancomycin. Cefazolin is used only for mild penicillin allergy.
key_points: Indications: positive 35-37 week swab, GBS bacteriuria, previous GBS infant, fever, ROM >= 18 h
First line: intravenous benzylpenicillin 3 g load then 1.8 g every 4 hours
Mild penicillin allergy: intravenous cefazolin
Severe penicillin allergy with clindamycin-susceptible isolate: intravenous clindamycin
Severe penicillin allergy with resistant or unknown isolate: intravenous vancomycin
explanation.pearls: Severe penicillin allergy and a clindamycin-susceptible GBS isolate get intravenous clindamycin 600 mg every eight hours; if the isolate is resistant or unknown, default to intravenous vancomycin rather than cefazolin.

subtopic_detail: Pregnancy of uncertain viability and Anti-D
stem:
A 25-year-old G1P0 at 7+2 weeks by last menstrual period presents to early pregnancy unit with 24 hours of light vaginal bleeding and mild lower abdominal cramps. She has had no syncopal episodes and no shoulder tip pain. Her blood group on a booking blood test today is documented as RhD negative.

On examination her observations are normal. Speculum reveals a closed cervical os with a small amount of dark blood pooling in the posterior fornix. Bimanual examination shows a soft anteverted uterus, no cervical motion tenderness, and no adnexal mass or tenderness. There has been no previous miscarriage and she conceived spontaneously. There is no significant past gynaecological history and she takes no medications.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse rate 84, blood pressure 116/72, respiratory rate 16, SpO2 99 percent on room air, temperature 36.7 degrees
data_table.Investigations: serum beta-hCG 14000 IU/L, transvaginal ultrasound: single intrauterine gestational sac with yolk sac and embryo with crown-rump length 3.5 mm, no foetal cardiac activity demonstrated, no adnexal mass, no free fluid

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Admit for intravenous antibiotics and uterine evacuation in theatre for a presumed septic incomplete miscarriage with retained products
options.rationale: There is no fever, no offensive discharge, and no inflammatory markers raised. Septic management is not indicated and would be invasive on an indeterminate scan. (Source: RANZCOG: Miscarriage)

options.text: Administer intramuscular methotrexate 50 mg/m squared and arrange serial beta-hCG measurements every 48 hours for one week
options.rationale: Methotrexate is used for tubal ectopic pregnancy meeting RANZCOG medical management criteria. An intrauterine sac is visible on ultrasound, making ectopic unlikely and methotrexate contraindicated. (Source: RANZCOG: Miscarriage)

options.text: Discharge home with repeat transvaginal ultrasound and beta-hCG in 7 to 10 days, give Anti-D 250 IU intramuscular, and provide written safety netting advice
options.rationale: An embryo with crown-rump length less than 7 mm without cardiac activity is indeterminate, not miscarriage; repeat scan in 7 to 10 days is required. RANZCOG recommends 250 IU Anti-D for any RhD-negative woman with first-trimester bleeding. (Source: RANZCOG: Miscarriage)

options.text: Commence vaginal misoprostol 800 micrograms now and a second dose at 24 hours for medical management of a missed miscarriage
options.rationale: Medical evacuation requires the diagnostic threshold for miscarriage to be met (crown-rump length 7 mm or more without cardiac activity, or mean sac diameter 25 mm or more empty). The current scan is indeterminate. (Source: RANZCOG: Miscarriage)

options.text: Admit for 48 hour observation and offer dilatation and curettage if vaginal bleeding has not stopped by review the following morning
options.rationale: Inpatient observation is not standard for haemodynamically stable threatened miscarriage. Surgical evacuation is offered only once miscarriage is confirmed and after patient counselling. (Source: RANZCOG: Miscarriage)

explanation.summary: RANZCOG diagnostic criteria for first-trimester miscarriage on transvaginal ultrasound require either a crown-rump length of 7 mm or more without cardiac activity, or a mean gestational sac diameter of 25 mm or more without a visible embryo. Below these cut-offs the scan is indeterminate and warrants a repeat in 7 to 10 days. Any RhD-negative woman with first-trimester bleeding receives 250 IU Anti-D within 72 hours.
key_points: CRL >= 7 mm without cardiac activity = miscarriage diagnostic
Mean sac diameter >= 25 mm without embryo = miscarriage diagnostic
Below thresholds: repeat ultrasound in 7-10 days
Anti-D 250 IU intramuscular for RhD-negative first trimester bleeding
Beta-hCG should approximately double every 48 hours in viable intrauterine pregnancy
explanation.pearls: The diagnostic cut-off for miscarriage on transvaginal ultrasound is a crown-rump length of 7 mm without cardiac activity; anything smaller is an indeterminate scan and the rest plan is repeat ultrasound in 7 to 10 days.

stem:
A 32-year-old G2P1 at 35+2 weeks self-presents to triage with two hours of frontal headache, blurry vision with scotomata, and epigastric pain. Her booking blood pressure was 118/74 and her antenatal record shows no prior hypertension. Foetal movements have been normal today.

On examination she is alert and orientated. There is mild hyperreflexia at the knees without clonus. There is no peripheral oedema and no rash. Urine dipstick is 3+ protein. Cardiotocograph shows a baseline of 140 with normal variability and no decelerations. Speculum is not yet performed. Her observation chart is shown below. There is no abdominal tenderness on uterine palpation between contractions and no PV bleeding.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse rate 102, blood pressure 168/112, respiratory rate 20, SpO2 99 percent on room air, temperature 36.8 degrees
data_table.Investigations: haemoglobin 124 g/L (115-145), platelets 142 x10^9/L (150-400), ALT 80 U/L (<35), urate 0.42 mmol/L (<0.36), creatinine 78 micromol/L (45-90), urine protein:creatinine ratio 220 mg/mmol

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Oral nifedipine 20 mg now, repeat in thirty minutes if blood pressure is not below 150/100, and arrange emergency caesarean section under general anaesthesia
options.rationale: Oral nifedipine is acceptable for acute blood pressure control but emergency caesarean under general anaesthesia is not the next step before seizure prophylaxis is commenced and the patient is stabilised. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Intravenous labetalol infusion alone and tocolysis with nifedipine to prolong gestation by 48 hours for corticosteroid completion
options.rationale: Tocolysis is not used in severe pre-eclampsia; the focus is maternal seizure prophylaxis and stabilisation prior to controlled delivery. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Oral methyldopa 250 mg three times daily and outpatient antenatal day-assessment unit review in twenty four hours for blood pressure trend
options.rationale: Methyldopa is too slow in onset for severe pre-eclampsia and outpatient review is unsafe with blood pressure 168/112 and neurological symptoms. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Intravenous diazepam 10 mg slow bolus for seizure prophylaxis and intravenous hydralazine 5 mg boluses every twenty minutes
options.rationale: Diazepam is not the recommended seizure prophylaxis or treatment in pre-eclampsia; magnesium sulfate is superior in both prevention and treatment of eclamptic seizures per the MAGPIE trial. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Intravenous magnesium sulfate 4 g loading over 20 minutes then 1 g/hour infusion plus intravenous labetalol for blood pressure control, and admit
options.rationale: Severe pre-eclampsia (blood pressure 160/110 or more plus neurological or epigastric symptoms or end-organ involvement) is the SOMANZ threshold for magnesium sulfate seizure prophylaxis at 4 g load then 1 g/hour, with concurrent antihypertensive treatment. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

explanation.summary: Severe pre-eclampsia is defined by SOMANZ as blood pressure 160/110 or more, end-organ dysfunction (hepatic, renal, neurological, haematological, placental, or pulmonary oedema), or persistent severe symptoms. Magnesium sulfate seizure prophylaxis (4 g intravenous load over 20 minutes then 1 g/hour for 24 hours) is indicated. Antihypertensive treatment runs in parallel (labetalol, nifedipine, or hydralazine). Delivery is the definitive treatment after maternal stabilisation.
key_points: SOMANZ severe pre-eclampsia: BP 160/110+ or end-organ damage or severe symptoms
Magnesium sulfate 4 g load over 20 min then 1 g/hour for 24 hours
Target BP under 160/110 acutely with labetalol, nifedipine, or hydralazine
Watch for magnesium toxicity: reflex loss, respiratory depression, oliguria
Delivery is the definitive treatment after stabilisation
explanation.pearls: The SOMANZ trigger for intravenous magnesium sulfate seizure prophylaxis is severe pre-eclampsia (BP 160/110 plus headache, visual symptoms, epigastric pain, or end-organ derangement), not eclampsia itself.

subtopic_detail: Preterm prelabour rupture of membranes at 32 weeks
stem:
A 28-year-old G1P0 at 32+1 weeks presents to delivery suite four hours after a sudden gush of clear fluid at home. She has no contractions. Her pregnancy has been low risk and she is fully vaccinated including booster pertussis. Her 35-week vaginal-rectal swab is not yet due. Booking blood group is A positive.

On examination she is well and afebrile. Speculum demonstrates clear amniotic pooling in the posterior fornix with a closed cervix. There is no offensive odour and the umbilical cord is not visible. Foetal cardiotocograph shows baseline 138 with normal variability, no contractions, and no decelerations. Ultrasound estimates the foetus is in a cephalic presentation with normal liquor outside the membrane defect, and weight on the 60th centile. She has no allergy.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse rate 86, blood pressure 112/70, respiratory rate 16, SpO2 99 percent on room air, temperature 36.8 degrees
data_table.Investigations: white cells 11.0 x10^9/L (4-11), CRP 8 mg/L (<5), high vaginal swab pending, urinalysis nil active

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Induction of labour with vaginal misoprostol now and intravenous benzylpenicillin given the unknown group B streptococcus status
options.rationale: Routine immediate induction at 32 weeks in well preterm prelabour rupture of membranes increases neonatal morbidity from prematurity. Expectant management to 34 weeks is preferred in stable women. (Source: RANZCOG: Term Prelabour Rupture of Membranes and PPROM)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Term Prelabour Rupture of Membranes and PPROM

options.text: Admit and give oral erythromycin 250 mg six hourly for ten days with antenatal betamethasone twice and expectant management to thirty four weeks
options.rationale: RANZCOG recommends admission, antenatal corticosteroids (betamethasone 11.4 mg intramuscular two doses 24 hours apart) before 34 weeks, prophylactic antibiotics (10 days oral erythromycin) to prolong latency and reduce neonatal sepsis (ORACLE I), and expectant management to 34 weeks if no contraindication. (Source: RANZCOG: Term Prelabour Rupture of Membranes and PPROM)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Term Prelabour Rupture of Membranes and PPROM

options.text: Outpatient management with twice weekly cardiotocograph reviews and serial inflammatory marker checks until thirty six weeks gestation
options.rationale: Preterm prelabour rupture of membranes requires inpatient observation for chorioamnionitis, abruption, and cord prolapse. Outpatient management is not standard in Australia. (Source: RANZCOG: Term Prelabour Rupture of Membranes and PPROM)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Term Prelabour Rupture of Membranes and PPROM

options.text: Tocolysis with nifedipine for 48 hours plus betamethasone, then discharge home for outpatient maternal-foetal medicine review
options.rationale: Tocolysis is not recommended in preterm prelabour rupture of membranes because it does not improve outcomes and risks masking infection; the patient also requires inpatient surveillance. (Source: RANZCOG: Term Prelabour Rupture of Membranes and PPROM)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Term Prelabour Rupture of Membranes and PPROM

options.text: Emergency caesarean section now for imminent cord prolapse risk with immediate neonatal intensive care unit admission planning
options.rationale: The cord is not visible and the foetus is cephalic with a normal cardiotocograph. Emergency caesarean is not indicated. (Source: RANZCOG: Term Prelabour Rupture of Membranes and PPROM)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Term Prelabour Rupture of Membranes and PPROM

explanation.summary: Preterm prelabour rupture of membranes between 24 and 34 weeks is managed inpatient with antenatal corticosteroids (betamethasone 11.4 mg intramuscular at 24 hour intervals for two doses), prophylactic erythromycin 250 mg orally six hourly for 10 days to prolong latency and reduce neonatal sepsis (ORACLE I), and expectant management to 34 weeks unless chorioamnionitis, abruption, or non-reassuring foetal status develops. Magnesium sulfate for neuroprotection is given if delivery becomes imminent before 30 weeks.
key_points: Admit for inpatient surveillance, twice daily observations and CTG
Antenatal betamethasone 11.4 mg intramuscular two doses 24 hours apart before 34 weeks
Oral erythromycin 250 mg six hourly for 10 days (ORACLE I)
Expectant management to 34 weeks unless chorioamnionitis or non-reassuring CTG
Magnesium sulfate neuroprotection if delivery imminent before 30 weeks
explanation.pearls: ORACLE I demonstrated that 10 days of oral erythromycin in preterm prelabour rupture of membranes prolongs latency and reduces neonatal sepsis, while co-amoxiclav increases necrotising enterocolitis risk and is avoided.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Term Prelabour Rupture of Membranes and PPROM
